name: Radium
name_origin: From the Latin 'radius' meaning 'ray', after the intense radioactivity it emits.

discovery.discoverer: Pierre Curie, Marie Curie, Gustave Bemont

occurrence.abundance_summary: Found in trace amounts in uranium and thorium ores; about one part in 10^12 by mass in Earth's crust. Pitchblende (uraninite) is the principal source.

compounds.name: Radium chloride
compounds.summary: First radium compound prepared in pure form by Marie Curie; used to isolate radium by fractional crystallization from barium chloride.

compounds.name: Radium bromide
compounds.summary: Glows faintly blue from self-radiation; historically used in luminous paints and early radiotherapy preparations.

compounds.name: Radium sulfate
compounds.summary: Among the most insoluble sulfates known; useful for separating radium from solutions containing barium.

uses: Historically used in luminous paint for clock and instrument dials (now banned due to severe health risks)
Early 20th-century cancer radiotherapy (largely replaced by safer isotopes like cobalt-60)
Neutron source when mixed with beryllium (Ra-Be neutron sources)
Scientific reference standard for radioactivity (the curie unit was originally based on radium-226)
reactivity_summary: A highly reactive alkaline earth metal that tarnishes rapidly in air, forming a black nitride layer, and reacts vigorously with water to release hydrogen and form radium hydroxide. Its chemistry closely resembles that of barium.
isotopes_summary: All isotopes are radioactive. Radium-226 (half-life 1600 years) is the most stable and most abundant in nature, decaying by alpha emission to radon-222. Other natural isotopes include Ra-223, Ra-224, and Ra-228.

citations.source: IUPAC Periodic Table of the Elements
CRC Handbook of Chemistry and Physics, 102nd ed.
